33370: Glioblastomas are very serious brain tumors that have different types of cells, with some cells called glioblastoma stem cells (GSCs) that can keep reproducing. These GSCs are controlled by different rules compared to other cells in the tumor. We found that a protein called A20, which helps cells survive and controls something called NF-kappaB, is more common in GSCs than in other tumor cells. To see if A20 is important for GSCs, we used a special virus to lower the amount of A20 in these cells. When we did this, the GSCs grew and survived less well. This happened because the cells didn't go through their normal growth stages as much and because they had less of a protein called p65/RelA. Having more A20 made GSCs less likely to die when exposed to a substance called TNFalpha, but taking away A20 made them more sensitive to this substance. Fewer GSCs surviving meant they couldn't reproduce as much in tests where we tried to grow new tumors. Targeting A20 also made it harder for the tumors to grow in mice with human brain tumors. Looking at information from people with glioblastomas showed that having too much A20 might be linked to better outcomes. 123859:
Scientists used a special camera to watch tiny kidney cells called podocytes and their neighbors in living mice over several days. They made the cells glow so they could see them better. When one kidney pipe got blocked, some podocytes grouped together and moved into a new area. By watching different colored glowing cells, they saw that multiple podocytes moved at the same time. They also noticed that some supporting cells connected to podocytes through tiny tubes. This shows that the kidney environment is very active and changing, not just static. This new way of watching cells could help us learn more about how kidneys get hurt and heal.

306006:
The strength of T cell activation depends on how well they form a special contact point called an immunological synapse. When T cells are activated, it's because their receptor connects with specific molecules. We found that a particular protein fragment can make T cells grow more than expected. Computer models suggested this happens because the fragment can't create a key structure needed for full activation. Tests confirmed that improving this structure makes the protein fragment less effective at activating T cells. This shows that many different things work together to decide how strong a T cell response will be.

464511:
Memory and Different Outcomes in Cell Choices Cells that start the same way and live together can look very different. It's not always clear why they look different - it could be random, outside influences, or the cells trying to make their own choices. To understand this better, we watched thousands of these cells for many generations without changing anything. We looked at how they chose between moving around alone and sticking together in chains. We found that when they move alone, there's no way to predict how long they'll do it - it just happens randomly. But when they stick together, they stay together for a set amount of time, which helps them work together. The system that decides whether they move or stick together is made up of three parts that work separately. When something makes them want to stick together, it can lead to forming a group called a biofilm. This means the cells can try out sticking together on their own before deciding if they really want to do it based on outside influences.

791050:
The study looked at whether breathing in tiny air particles from the past might make women more anxious today. We studied over 71,000 nurses across the US who filled out a special form about their anxiety levels in 2004. These nurses were mostly middle-aged to older women, with about 15% saying they felt very anxious. We measured how much tiny air particles they breathed in by looking at their homes' distance from busy roads and how much pollution was around them in the month, three months, six months, a year, and 15 years before they took the anxiety test. We found that the more tiny air particles people breathed in, especially in the last month, the more likely they were to feel very anxious. The risk seemed to go up by about 1 in 10 for every extra 10 tiny air particles they breathed in on average over a month. The study suggests that breathing in tiny air particles from the past might be linked to feeling more anxious now.
To determine if past exposure to tiny air particles could lead to more anxiety, we studied over 71,000 nurses across the U.S. who completed an anxiety survey in 2004. Most participants were middle-aged or older women, with about 15% reporting high anxiety levels. We measured their exposure to tiny air particles by considering their distance from busy roads and the amount of pollution around them in the month, three months, six months, a year, and 15 years before the survey. Our findings showed that higher exposure to these tiny particles, especially in the last month, was linked to a greater chance of having high anxiety. For every 10 more tiny air particles breathed in on average over a month, the risk of high anxiety increased by about 1 in 10. This suggests that breathing in tiny air particles from the past may be related to feeling more anxious now.
970012:
Cold exposure makes heart problems worse and can make them more dangerous. When it's cold, even without eating more, fat in the body starts to break down faster. This causes smaller pieces of bad cholesterol to appear in the blood. In mice that don't have certain proteins needed for healthy hearts, being cold for a long time leads to more buildup of fatty deposits in their arteries. These deposits grow bigger and become unstable, which is risky. Removing a special protein called UCP1, found in a type of fat called brown fat, stops these harmful effects in mice without the heart-healthy proteins. Being cold also lowers a protein called adiponectin, and giving more of this protein to mice helps prevent the buildup of fatty deposits. These results help explain why cold weather might be bad for people's hearts.

1049501:
Neutrophil extracellular traps, which are like nets made by white blood cells, contain damaged parts of mitochondria. These traps can cause the body to attack itself and lead to symptoms similar to lupus. We found that these traps need special chemicals called mitochondrial reactive oxygen species to form properly. When white blood cells are stimulated by certain substances, their mitochondria change shape and move to the outside of the cell. The damaged parts of mitochondria released outside the cell can make the immune system more active. Injecting these damaged parts into mice causes the immune system to produce a chemical called interferon, which can trigger an autoimmune response. People with certain conditions that affect their mitochondria also show signs of producing these damaging traps and having high levels of interferon. By stopping the production of these special chemicals from mitochondria, we could reduce the severity of autoimmune symptoms in mice. Overall, our research shows that mitochondria play a key role in making both the damaging traps and the immune system overreact in autoimmune diseases.

1084345:
Restoring a process called chaperone-mediated autophagy in older liver helps keep cells healthy and working well. Chaperone-mediated autophagy is a way cells get rid of old or damaged proteins. In older animals, this process slows down, leading to more damaged proteins inside cells and problems with how organs work. To see if we could stop these problems from happening, we made special mice that could keep their chaperone-mediated autophagy working even as they got older. By doing this, we found that the mice had fewer damaged proteins inside their cells, were better at dealing with protein damage, and their livers worked better. This suggests that keeping this process active might help prevent some of the issues that come with getting older.

1122279:
Exercise can help blood vessels in the heart relax better when they are connected to collateral blood flow. In some hearts, these blood vessels don't relax as well as they should. We wanted to see if exercising could improve how these vessels work. We did an experiment on pigs where we blocked one part of their heart's blood supply and let them grow new ways to get blood. Then we divided the pigs into two groups - one that exercised regularly on a treadmill and another that stayed inactive. After a while, we looked at the tiny blood vessels in their hearts. The vessels that got less blood didn't relax as well in the inactive group compared to those in the active group. But in the group that exercised, the vessels worked just as well whether they got less blood or not. Ni

1148122:
Understanding how organisms change to better survive in certain conditions is a big question in science. It's hard to figure out exactly what changes happen inside them because many parts can be affected, and each part might not change much. We created a way to look at both experiments and computer models together to solve this problem. We used this to learn about how E. coli bacteria become more resistant to alcohol. We tested small changes in their genes one at a time while they were exposed to alcohol. Then, using computers, we looked at how these changes fit together to form bigger processes in the cell, like adjusting to changes in water levels or making the cell wall stronger. Surprisingly, we found that a key way they adapt is by changing how they process and use alcohol inside their cells. By studying the changes in the bacteria that can survive alcohol better in the lab, we learned about natural ways they can protect themselves. These bacteria mostly followed similar paths to become more alcohol-resistant, just like what we predicted from looking at all possible changes in a simplified way.

1180972:
A study looked at how genes affect being overweight in grown-ups who were adopted away from their birth parents as babies. We compared these adopted grown-ups with their brothers and sisters who stayed with their birth parents. The adopted grown-ups were grouped based on their weight - thin, normal, overweight, or very overweight. We measured the weight and height of 115 full brothers and sisters of 57 adopted grown-ups and 850 half brothers and sisters of 341 adopted grown-ups. The full brothers and sisters tended to have higher weights if their adopted sibling was heavier. For half brothers and sisters, there was a smaller increase in weight as their adopted sibling got heavier. There were no big differences based on whether the adopted grown-ups or their brothers and sisters were boys or girls. However, full brothers and sisters of adopted grown-ups who were overweight or very overweight had much higher weights. This suggests that genes play a role in how heavy people are, even when they live in the same home, and this can happen without being affected by gender, including both small and large genetic influences on being overweight.

1215116:
Over the last 20 years, there have been big improvements in controlling some major tropical illnesses in Africa [1]. This has led to fewer people getting sick from diseases like lymphatic filariasis, onchocerciasis, guinea worm, leprosy, and trachoma [2]. These diseases are common in poor, rural areas where people live in low-income countries [3]. For centuries, these illnesses have caused suffering and were often seen as shameful [3]. Now, thanks to special programs aimed at stopping these diseases, it's possible that we could almost get rid of them in some places [2-8]. For example, we might even stop guinea worm completely [9]. These diseases are old and have been around for a long time, affecting many people [3]. They make life harder for poor people and can cause shame [3]. But now, because of successful efforts to control them, there's hope that we can stop these illnesses from spreading in some areas [2-8].

1469751:
A special type of tiny ball called aptamer-functionalized lipid nanoparticles was created to target bone-building cells called osteoblasts. This was done to deliver a specific piece of genetic information, called Plekho1 siRNA, directly to these cells. We used a method called cell-SELEX to find a special molecule called aptamer CH6 that can stick to osteoblasts. When we put this aptamer on the tiny balls along with the genetic information, it helped the genetic information get into the osteoblasts more easily. This led to less of a protein called Plekho1 being made inside the bone-building cells. With less Plekho1, the bones grew stronger and healthier in both animals with weak bones and healthy animals. This shows that using these tiny balls with the special molecule can be a new way to help build stronger bones, making the delivery of genetic information much more precise at the level of individual cells.

1471041:
In people with celiac disease, their intestines make lots of antibodies against a protein called transglutaminase 2 (TG2) when they eat gluten. These antibodies are made by special cells called plasma cells. We studied these plasma cells directly from the intestines of people with active celiac disease. We found that there are many more of these TG2-fighting plasma cells than usual in the small intestine. The antibodies these cells make are very good at sticking to TG2, but they don't change much after being made. Unlike what happens when you get an infection, these cells haven't been making new copies of themselves recently, and they can live for a long time outside the body. This suggests that the body has a ready-made set of antibodies that can quickly react to TG2. When these antibodies are made as certain types (IgD or IgM), they can be stuck together by TG2, but not when they're made as other types (IgA1 or IgG1). This might mean that the body uses a special group of B cells (which make antibodies) to make these antibodies, which could explain why the response to TG2 looks like it's happening for the first time.

1568684:
The compound chenodeoxycholic acid makes human brown fat more active. Scientists are interested in brown fat because it could help fight diseases related to how our body uses energy. In mice, a certain type of fat called brown fat becomes more active when they eat something called bile acids. Bile acids work by turning on a special protein in brown fat cells that helps them burn more energy. We wanted to see if giving people chenodeoxycholic acid, a kind of bile acid, would make their brown fat more active. After taking chenodeoxycholic acid for two days, 12 women had more active brown fat. Their bodies also used more energy overall. When we put chenodeoxycholic acid or special chemicals that act like bile acids into human brown fat cells in the lab, the cells started burning more energy and making more of the special protein. This didn't happen in white fat cells. These results suggest that bile acids might be a way to make brown fat more active in people. ```

1606628:
In 1990 and 2015, we looked at how many young kids around the world were too small for their age due to not getting enough food. The United Nations had a goal to cut this number in half by 2015. We wanted to see how this problem changed in different parts of the world over time. We used information from about 31 million children under 5 years old who took part in health surveys in 139 countries from 1965 to 2002. By using special math, we found that globally, the number of underweight kids went down from about 26.5% in 1990 to 17.6% in 2015. This means there were fewer kids who weren't getting enough food. In richer countries, the number dropped from 1.6% to 0.9%. In poorer areas, it went down from 30.2% to 19.3%. Overall, there was progress, but more work needs to be done to help all kids grow healthy.

1642727:
The study looked at how exercise might help older people who are having trouble with their memory but aren't diagnosed with Alzheimer's. We wanted to see if being more active could slow down how much their thinking skills get worse. We did a big experiment where we asked 170 people to either keep doing what they normally did or start exercising at home for 24 weeks. Then we checked how well they could remember things and think clearly after 18 months. People who exercised showed less decline in their thinking skills compared to those who didn't change their routine. But the difference wasn't very big.

1805641: In areas where malaria is common, a type of medicine called Artemisinin Combination Therapy (ACT) can help reduce how much the disease spreads. This study looked at how well ACT and other medicines might work in six places with different levels of malaria. To do this, researchers created a computer model to show what might happen if these medicines were used. They used information from a survey of over 5,000 people to estimate how often people got sick or had the disease without showing symptoms. The goal was to see how well different medicines could stop the spread of malaria by making infected people less likely to pass the disease to mosquitoes. To understand this, they studied how these medicines affected the number of malaria cells in people's blood and how likely those cells were to make mosquitoes sick. In summary, the study aimed to find out which medicines could best control malaria in different areas by looking at how well they worked in reducing the spread of the disease. 1834762: Commensal and disease-causing bacteria living in our bodies can affect how much we weigh, our risk of getting cancer, and whether we develop autoimmune diseases. Scientists discovered that a part of these bacteria, called curli, which forms when bacteria stick together, can join with the bacteria's own genetic material, DNA. This joining speeds up the formation of curli into long strands and creates complex structures that can activate parts of our immune system. Specifically, these structures can make special immune cells called dendritic cells produce substances known as cytokines, which can be harmful in a condition called lupus. When researchers gave these curli-DNA structures to mice that are prone to developing lupus or to regular mice, it caused their immune systems to become more active and start making antibodies against their own body. Additionally, when lupus-prone mice were infected with bacteria that could make curli, they produced more antibodies than when infected with bacteria that couldn't make curli. This research suggests that the mix of bacteria in our gut and certain types of bacterial infections might play a role in causing lupus and other autoimmune conditions, and it points to curli as a possible target for new treatments.
1897324:
A study using gene tests found that an LKB1–MARK signaling path controls how the Hippo–YAP pathway works. The Hippo–YAP pathway helps manage stem cell behavior and organ size. To find parts of this pathway, scientists used a special test on human cells. This test showed some new kinases (a type of protein) that control different cell activities. One of these, LKB1, is a protein that usually stops tumors but its full role isn't clear yet. We found that LKB1 works by affecting other proteins called MARKs, which help move a key cell part called Scribble and control the main Hippo kinases. Our results also suggest that a protein called YAP is important for LKB1's tumor-fighting abilities. This means that when LKB1 doesn't work right, it can lead to YAP being active, which might cause cancer. Our findings show how LKB1 and YAP are linked and could help in treating cancers where LKB1 isn't working properly. They also give us clues about what starts the Hippo–YAP signaling process.

1982286: Reverse engineering of the genetic control system that drives TLX oncogenes in T cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia (T-ALL) reveals that TLX1 and TLX3 are major controllers of the cancer-causing network. By analyzing how these genes interact, we found that TLX1 and TLX3 work together to manage a network that leads to T-ALL. A closer look at this network showed that RUNX1 is a crucial player in the cancer caused by TLX1 and TLX3, and suggests that RUNX1 normally helps prevent T cells from turning into cancer cells. Our findings also show that RUNX1 is often damaged in human cases of T-ALL, supporting the idea that it acts as a tumor-suppressor gene. In summary, our study highlights the importance of TLX1 and TLX3 in driving T-ALL, demonstrates the effectiveness of network analysis in uncovering key players in cancer, and identifies RUNX1 as a gene that stops T cells from becoming cancerous in T-ALL. TLX1 and TLX3 are like the main switches in a complex machine that causes T-ALL. By studying how these switches work, we found out that RUNX1 is a critical part of the machine and usually helps keep T cells healthy. We also found that RUNX1 is often broken in people with T-ALL, which means it's likely a gene that protects against cancer in T cells. Overall, our research shows how important TLX1 and TLX3 are in causing T-ALL, how useful it is to look at networks of genes to understand cancer, and that RUNX1 is a gene that keeps T cells from turning into cancer in T-ALL. 2014909: Oncogenic signals from cancer cells attract special immune cells called myeloid-derived suppressor cells (MDSCs) to help start tumors. These immune cells play a big part in both the growth of the original tumor and any spreading to other parts of the body. Even though people with the same type of cancer can have very different levels of these immune cells, scientists don't fully understand why.
2095573: LDL cholesterol levels: a big study looking at genes BACKGROUND LDL cholesterol is linked to heart problems. Knowing more about how our bodies make and control LDL cholesterol could help find new ways to treat it. So, we did a big study to see which genes are connected to LDL cholesterol levels. METHODS We looked at information from up to 11,685 people who had their LDL cholesterol measured. We checked over 293,000 spots on their DNA that happen often enough to be important. We also used data from another set of tests on up to 4,337 people. Then, we checked if these results held true in two other groups of people. We used special math to find the most important spots and combined all the data to see how each spot affects LDL cholesterol levels. FINDINGS In our first look, we found two spots (called rs599839 and rs4970834) that strongly connect to LDL cholesterol levels on part of chromosome 1. Our second check found another spot that connects to LDL cholesterol levels.
2119889:
Characterizing two types of tiny chemical blockers that stop a specific group of proteins from making new thread-like structures called actin filaments. These proteins, known as Arp2/3 complex, help cells move in many ways. Scientists want to know how much these proteins contribute to the way nerve cells grow and find paths, but it's hard to study them without simple ways to stop them from working inside living cells. We found two kinds of tiny chemicals that can do this. One stops the proteins from moving properly, and the other changes their shape. Both prevent certain bacteria and immune cells from making long, tail-like structures made of actin filaments. Having two different ways to stop these proteins gives scientists a strong tool to learn more about them in living cells.

2177022: Immune cells called dendritic cells move in certain ways based on chemical signals called chemokines. These signals can make cells move in a straight line or randomly, and can also help cells stick to surfaces. We studied how dendritic cells move in response to different types of chemokines. When the chemokine CCL21 was stuck to a surface, it made dendritic cells move randomly but only on that surface because it helped them stick. When dendritic cells touched CCL21 directly, they cut off the part that sticks to the surface, allowing the chemokine to float freely in the liquid around them. This free-floating chemokine, like another similar signal called CCL19, made cells move towards it but didn't let them stick to surfaces. The combination of sticking and moving towards signals helps dendritic cells move in a controlled way, much like they do in parts of the body where immune responses are coordinated. 2251426:
MicroRNAs: A Safety Net Against Stress? Recent findings show that microRNAs (miRNAs) play a key role in helping cells deal with stress, beyond their known job in cell development. Interestingly, miRNAs, which usually turn down the production of certain molecules, can sometimes turn up the production during stressful times. This might happen because miRNAs team up with other molecules in new ways when the cell is under stress, causing these molecules to move around inside the cell differently than usual.

2356950: MBD1, a protein that controls how genes are turned on or off, helps manage how neural stem cells grow and change into other types of brain cells. In our previous study, we found that not having enough MBD1 makes it hard for these special brain cells to develop properly, but we didn't know why. Now, we've discovered that MBD1 controls the production of a small piece of genetic material called miR-184. When there's too much miR-184, it makes neural stem cells grow more but prevents them from turning into different brain cells. By stopping miR-184, we can fix the problems caused by not having enough MBD1. We also learned that miR-184 affects another gene called Numbl, which is important for brain development. MiR-184 does this by attaching to Numbl's instructions and changing how they're used. We found that adding extra Numbl can correct the issues caused by too much miR-184 or not enough MBD1. So, MBD1, miR-184, and Numbl work together to help neural stem cells decide whether to grow or turn into different brain cells. 2425364:
The connection between how much vitamin D mom has in her blood during pregnancy and how the baby grows and is born: A big review of many studies. We looked at 3357 studies and found 31 that were useful. Low amounts of vitamin D in mom's blood linked to a higher chance of the mom having high blood sugar during pregnancy (gestational diabetes), high blood pressure during pregnancy (pre-eclampsia), and the baby being smaller than expected for how far along the pregnancy is (small for gestational age). The more vitamin D mom has, the less likely these problems are to happen.
To assess the impact of vitamin D levels in mom's blood during pregnancy on how the baby grows and is born, we did a big review of many studies. We checked 3,357 studies and found 31 that were helpful. We saw that low levels of vitamin D in mom's blood were linked to a higher risk of the mom having high blood sugar during pregnancy (gestational diabetes), high blood pressure during pregnancy (pre-eclampsia), and the baby being smaller than expected for how far along the pregnancy is (small for gestational age). Having more vitamin D in mom's blood seemed to lower the chances of these problems happening.
2565138: Hyaluronic acid makes it hard for drugs to reach tumors and affects how blood flows in a type of cancer called pancreatic cancer. In this study, scientists used a special mouse with pancreatic cancer to see if they could remove hyaluronic acid to help drugs work better. They gave the mice a treatment that breaks down hyaluronic acid and then looked at how well blood could flow to the tumor and how well drugs could get inside the tumor. They also tested if combining this treatment with another drug called gemcitabine could help stop the cancer from growing. The results showed that removing hyaluronic acid helped blood flow better and allowed more drugs to reach the tumor. Combining this treatment with gemcitabine made the tumor shrink and helped the mice live longer. This suggests that removing hyaluronic acid might be a good way to help treat pancreatic cancer. The scientists think that breaking down hyaluronic acid could be a new approach to fighting this type of cancer. 2831620:
Protein that gets added or removed with acetic acid can change how it works, and this is part of how our bodies control things like energy use and growth. Changes in these proteins might lead to problems like being overweight, having trouble with blood sugar, and heart issues. Scientists are learning more about these changes in proteins and how they affect different parts of our health, including how we fight off infections and how our brains work. This summary looks at what we know about how these protein changes affect how our bodies use energy and deal with diseases related to how we use energy.

Scientists are studying how these changes in proteins can also affect other aspects of our health, such as fighting off infections and brain function. This review focuses on understanding how these protein changes impact energy use and relate to diseases that affect energy metabolism.
2988714:
Local making and breaking down of proteins control how the tip of a growing nerve changes shape in response to signals. Only certain proteins are made at the tip, while others travel from the cell body. We wanted to know why just some proteins are made there. We found that when the tip gets signals, it starts breaking down a key protein called RhoA, which helps the tip change shape. If we stop the breakdown of RhoA, the need for making new proteins at the tip disappears. We also found that most of the proteins broken down at the tip are ones that are made there. So, breaking down these proteins is a big part of what happens at the tip, and it means new proteins must be made there to keep responding to signals.

3203590:
Unique ways for cells to react are formed when certain proteins in the cell work together. One type of protein, called RXR, often partners with other proteins to do their job. RXR can team up with two specific types of proteins, T3R and RAR, to form pairs. Scientists wanted to know if these pairs could respond to different signals. They created a way to look at how each protein works alone or when paired with RXR. They discovered that when RXR is paired with T3R or RAR, it doesn't work as well on its own. But RXR can still be active with another type of protein called NGFI-B/Nurr1, even without attaching to DNA. When RXR teams up with an always-active version of NGFI-B/Nurr1, they form a new pair that responds to hormones. This shows that when proteins team up, they can create new and different ways to react to signals. It suggests that how proteins interact is very important for how cells respond to different signals.

In a study looking at why some kids in Italy get very overweight, we checked the genes of 67 families where kids were found to be overweight and compared them to 112 families where kids were not overweight. We looked closely at how these families' genes might play a role in becoming overweight. Our findings show that genes do seem to be involved, and there's evidence that one gene, which is passed down from parents to children, may have a small impact on whether someone becomes overweight.

3441524:
Human TRPML1 channel shapes when it's open or closed TRPML1 is a special kind of channel that helps control the amount of calcium in parts of cells called lysosomes. When something goes wrong with TRPML1, it can cause a serious condition called mucolipidosis type IV. In this study, scientists used a technique called electron cryo-microscopy to look at the full human TRPML1 channel in two different states: one where it's closed and another where it's open after being activated by something. They noticed that certain parts of the channel, like some helices and pore helix 1, play a role in how the channel opens and closes. The scientists also found a special area inside the channel where a substance binds to make the channel open. This is different from what happens in another type of channel called TRPV1. Understanding how TRPML1 works could help us learn more about how it causes problems in mucolipidosis type IV and might lead to new ways to treat it.

3475317:
Inflammation in TB spots in people's lungs is arranged in a specific way. These spots, called granulomas, are a sign of tuberculosis. But we don't fully understand what they do or how they form. To learn more about granulomas, we looked at the proteins inside them without any preconceived ideas. We used special tools to carefully cut out parts of the granulomas, studied them with powerful machines, and took detailed pictures. We discovered that the center of these spots has a lot of inflammation, with substances that fight infection and cause swelling. The area around another part of the spot, called caseum, has less inflammation. This pattern was seen in six different people and in rabbits too. Even though the body tries to balance its responses to fight off TB, our findings show that these responses are separated within each spot. By looking at the proteins and fats in these spots from both people and animals, we think that the body's reaction to TB depends on where these inflammation-causing processes are located as the spot forms.

3553087:
Cigarette smoke can cause lung problems like bronchitis and emphysema in mice. We found that a protein called IRP2, which is more common in people with lung issues, plays a role in these problems. Mice without IRP2 did not get sick when exposed to cigarette smoke. By studying how genes work in the lungs, we learned that IRP2 helps control how much iron goes into mitochondria, the powerhouses of cells. Too much iron in mitochondria can make them not work well, leading to lung issues. Mice without enough of a protein called frataxin, which helps with iron, had trouble clearing mucus from their airways and more lung inflammation. Mice without enough of another protein, cytochrome c oxidase, which is part of mitochondria, did not have these problems. Giving mice medicine that removes extra iron from mitochondria or feeding them less iron helped protect them from lung problems caused by cigarette smoke. This treatment also improved their ability to clear mucus and reduced lung inflammation.

3863543:
Mesenchymal inflammation can cause damage to blood-forming stem cells and might predict how a disease changes over time in people with certain blood disorders. In a study using mice with a condition similar to a rare human disorder called Shwachman-Diamond syndrome, we found that problems in the supporting cells around the blood-forming cells led to issues like energy problems, harmful chemical reactions, and signs of damaged DNA. By looking closely at these supporting cells from both mice and humans with different blood disorders, we discovered that an inflammatory response involving specific proteins and signals was a key factor in causing this damage. This inflammatory response could help us understand if someone's condition is likely to get worse and lead to leukemia, especially in a condition called myelodysplastic syndrome. Overall, our results suggest that targeting this inflammatory response in the supportive tissue around blood-forming cells could be important for treating or preventing the progression of these diseases.

3898784:
In a study of over 140,000 people who had bleeding in their brain, researchers looked at whether those who were taking certain blood-thinning medicines before their brain bleed were more likely to die while in the hospital. The study found that out of all the patients, about 11% were taking an older type of blood thinner called warfarin, and about 3.5% were taking newer types called NOACs. Many patients also took other medicines to prevent blood clots. The study showed that the severity of the brain bleed didn't differ much between those who took warfarin, NOACs, or no blood thinners. However, the older and those with conditions like irregular heartbeat or past strokes were more common in both warfarin and NOAC groups.
In a study of over 141,311 people who had bleeding in their brain, researchers examined if those taking certain blood-thinning medicines before their brain bleed were more likely to die while in the hospital. Among these patients, about 10.6% were taking the older type of blood thinner called warfarin, and about 3.5% were taking newer types called NOACs. Many patients also took other medicines to prevent blood clots. The study showed that how severe the brain bleed was didn't differ much between those who took warfarin, NOACs, or no blood thinners. However, older age and having conditions like irregular heartbeat or past strokes were more common in both warfarin and NOAC groups.
4347374:
The human body has special defense cells that can stop viruses from spreading. One such cell uses a tool called APOBEC3G to fight off a virus called HIV. HIV needs a helper called Vif to protect itself from APOBEC3G. Without Vif, HIV cannot spread properly. APOBEC3G is similar to another tool called APOBEC1, which changes certain parts of a molecule to help the body. Scientists weren't sure if APOBEC3G could do something similar to stop HIV. But they found out that APOBEC3G can make lots of changes to HIV's genetic material when it's being copied, making the virus less able to spread. This defense works not just for HIV, but for many other similar viruses too. It seems the body has a general way to stop these kinds of viruses using this tool.

4350400: In plants, cells develop specific boundaries and communicate with each other through a process called polarity. Special proteins called PINs help move a signaling chemical named auxin around the plant, which is crucial for growth and development. PINs are like road signs directing the flow of auxin between cells. However, how these PINs become polarized (develop a specific direction) is not fully understood. In this study, we found that PIN polarization depends on a process called endocytosis, where the cell takes in and recycles parts of its membrane. When we interfere with this recycling process, PINs lose their directionality. This disruption affects how auxin is distributed during early plant development, leading to changes in how certain genes are turned on or off. These changes can cause leaves to start forming root-like structures, which is unusual. Our findings suggest that there are two steps involved in creating and maintaining PIN polarity. To sum up, we discovered how cells in plants organize themselves and communicate using a two-step process involving the movement and recycling of special proteins. 4381486: Haematopoietic stem cells are special types of cells that help make other blood cells. Some scientists thought these cells might keep old DNA parts and avoid dividing often. Others believed they could keep certain labels, like BrdU, which is a special chemical used to mark cells. But it wasn't clear if these ideas were true for the blood-making stem cells.
4387784:
Half the world's population has a chronic infection with a bacteria called Helicobacter pylori, which can cause stomach inflammation, ulcers, and an increased risk of stomach cancer. This bacteria needs a special door, called HpUreI, to let in a chemical called urea when the stomach is very acidic. When the door opens, it allows urea to quickly reach an enzyme inside the bacteria that turns it into ammonia and carbon dioxide. These products help neutralize the acid in the stomach, keeping the bacteria safe. We have now found out what this door looks like. It is made up of six parts arranged in a circle, with a fatty layer in the center. Each part has a tunnel formed by six curved pieces that go through the cell membrane. These tunnels have a unique shape with a special twist that hasn't been seen before. The tunnel and where the parts connect have certain important parts that are similar to other related doors. Most of the tunnel is lined with flat or waxy parts that create two narrow spots in the middle. Changing one of these spots, specifically a part called tryptophan 153, could affect how well the door works.

4388470:
Somatic sex identity is cell-autonomous in chickens. In mammals, embryos start as gender-neutral until a special gene tells them to develop into males or females. This idea is believed to work for all animals with backbones, but it hasn't been proven. We looked at three chickens that had parts of their bodies that looked like males and other parts that looked like females. This is very rare and natural. Our findings suggest that chicken body cells might already know if they are supposed to be male or female. To test this, we moved some cells from one embryo to another, making new embryos with mixed male and female parts. Unlike what happens in mammals, these mixed cells didn't stay in the main parts of the chicken's reproductive organs. For example, when we moved female cells into a male embryo, those cells kept their female characteristics and showed signs of being able to do female functions. This shows that chicken body cells have a built-in gender and that their development is mostly controlled by themselves.

4406819:
Bacteria have a special tool called the type VI secretion system (T6SS), which is like a long, flexible tail. This tail helps bacteria catch and kill other cells, including both bacteria and some types of tiny organisms called eukaryotes. Scientists aren't sure exactly what parts make up this tail or how it picks and sends its harmful substances. In this study, we found that certain proteins, which we call PAAR proteins, help shape the tip of the T6SS tail. These proteins also attach the harmful substances to the tail. By looking at the structure of these proteins, we learned they make the tip of the tail sharper. We discovered that these PAAR proteins are very important for the T6SS to work properly and for the bacteria to kill their targets. Our findings suggest a new way the T6SS might operate, where it uses the VgrG-PAAR tail to send multiple harmful substances all at once into the target cells.

4414547: Mosaic changes in a gene called PPM1D are linked to a higher risk of getting breast or ovarian cancer. Better tools for looking at our genes have made it possible to find these rare changes. But studying them is tricky. By using advanced gene sequencing on 507 important genes from 1,150 samples and then testing the results in 13,642 people, we found that certain rare changes in PPM1D are more common in people with breast or ovarian cancer compared to those without. Specifically, out of 7,781 people with breast cancer, 25 had these changes, while only 1 out of 5,861 people without cancer had them. We also found these changes in 6,912 people with breast cancer and 1,121 people with ovarian cancer. These changes were mostly found in a small part of the gene near the end. When we tested how these changes affect the gene's function, we saw that they make the gene work extra hard to suppress p53, which is important for protecting cells from damage. So even though these changes stop the gene from working properly, they might actually make it more active in a way that could increase the risk of cancer. In summary, we discovered that certain rare changes in the PPM1D gene are linked to a higher chance of developing breast or ovarian cancer. To understand this better, we need to look at how these changes affect the gene's function. Mosaic changes in a gene called PPM1D are linked to a higher risk of getting breast or ovarian cancer. 4423559:
Planar cell polarity signals help shape the developing nervous system during a critical stage called neurulation. Abnormalities in these signals can cause problems like neural tube defects, which affect about one in every thousand babies born. Previous studies in mice and frogs showed that a protein called Vangl2 and other related proteins might help guide the movement of early brain cells to the center of the embryo. Now, we've found something new about how these signals work in fish embryos. We discovered that these signals help align the early brain cells along the front-to-back direction of the embryo. This alignment is briefly lost when the cells divide but quickly returns as the new cells settle back into place. When the fish version of Vangl2 is missing, the cells lose their alignment, struggle to return to their proper positions, and form extra clusters of cells that can lead to neural tube defects. Interestingly, if we stop the cells from dividing, the overall shape of the neural tube can still be fixed, even though some cells are still misaligned. These findings show that these signals play a key role in linking cell division with the shaping of the nervous system, and suggest a new way that neural tube defects might develop.

4427392:
In the human body, special cells called cardiovascular progenitor cells grow into different types of cells that make up the heart. These include heart muscle cells, blood vessel cells, and cells that help form the walls of blood vessels. Previous research on mice showed that all these different types of cells come from a single group of early cells that can turn into any of these types. To see if something similar happens in humans, scientists studied how human stem cells, which can become many types of cells, develop into heart-related cells. They found that when they guided human stem cells to grow in specific conditions, they produced a group of cells that could become heart muscle, blood vessel, and blood vessel wall cells. When these cells were grown in flat layers, more than half of them turned into beating heart muscle cells. This suggests there are similar early cells in humans that can develop into the various parts of the heart and blood vessels.

4456756:
BDNF and its receptor TrkB are important for how brain cells change and learn. Scientists wanted to know if BDNF is released and TrkB is activated during a specific type of learning called structural long-term potentiation (sLTP). They used special tools to watch what happens inside tiny parts of brain cells called dendritic spines in mouse brains. When they triggered sLTP, they found that TrkB was quickly and continuously activated in the spines that were stimulated. This activation needed certain signals and newly made BDNF in the spines. They confirmed the presence of BDNF in the spines using another special tool. They also showed that BDNF can be released very quickly from a single spine when triggered by a chemical. Their results suggest that BDNF and TrkB work together in the spines to help with both the physical changes and the function of long-term memory. These findings show that each spine can make its own BDNF and use it to activate TrkB, which is important for learning.

4687948: HMG-CoA reductase inhibitors and the risk of broken hips in older people. RECENT FINDINGS Some studies on animals suggest that medicines used to lower cholesterol (called statins) might help build stronger bones, but it's not clear if these medicines actually make bones stronger or reduce the chance of breaking a bone in real people. GOAL To find out if taking statins could lower the risk of breaking a hip. STUDY TYPE We did a case-control study. WHERE AND WHO The study included 6,110 older adults from New Jersey who were 65 or older and were part of Medicare, and also got help from Medicaid or a special drug program for older people with disabilities. People who had surgery to fix a broken hip in 1994 were the cases, and we matched four other people without broken hips for every one person who had a broken hip based on their age and gender. WHAT WE MEASURED We looked at how likely someone was to break a hip if they took statins in the last six months or three years before their surgery, while considering things like their background, health conditions, and how often they visited doctors. RESULTS People who took statins in the last six months (risk reduced by 50%, with a range of 33% to 76%) or three years (risk reduced by 43%, with a range of 20% to 82%) before their surgery were less likely to break a hip compared to those who didn't take these medicines, even when we considered factors like their race, insurance, and other medications they might be taking.
4709641: Researchers are trying to find new medicines for Alzheimer's disease, which has shown some success in animals but failed in people. To better understand Alzheimer's, they used special brain cells made from people's skin cells. These cells were made to have a version of a protein called ApoE4, which is linked to a higher risk of getting Alzheimer's. They found that these cells had more of a substance called tau that gets stuck, even though they didn't make more of another substance called Aβ. The cells also showed signs of damage to a type of brain cell that uses a chemical called GABA. ApoE4 made more Aβ in human brain cells but not in mouse brain cells. Changing ApoE4 back to its normal form, ApoE3, fixed these problems, showing that ApoE4 was causing the issues. Cells without the ApoE protein acted like those with ApoE3, and adding ApoE4 caused the same harmful changes, suggesting that ApoE4 can cause problems on its own. Treating cells with ApoE4 with a special medicine that fixes the shape of ApoE4 helped reduce the harm, showing that changing the shape of ApoE4 could be a good way to treat Alzheimer's related to ApoE4.
4883040:
HIV weakens the immune system, making it easier for tuberculosis to develop, especially in places like sub-Saharan Africa. In 2010, about 1.1 million people got tuberculosis because they had HIV. Using medicine to fight HIV can help stop people from getting tuberculosis. We looked at studies to see how well these HIV medicines work in preventing tuberculosis in grown-ups who have HIV. We searched for studies in different databases and included those that compared how many people got tuberculosis whether or not they were taking HIV medicines in developing countries. The studies were divided into groups based on how many immune cells (CD4 cells) a person had when they started taking the HIV medicine. Eleven studies fit our criteria. The results show that taking HIV medicine can greatly reduce the chances of getting tuberculosis, no matter how many CD4 cells a person starts with. To be continued...

4942718:
High-speed tests found many new genes important for turning a common bacteria, Bacillus subtilis, into a resting state. Scientists have known about most of these genes for a long time, but recent studies showed more genes needed for this process. We used a special technique to see if there were any more unknown genes. We found 24 new genes that help make the resting state. By looking at how these genes affect the bacteria's growth stages, we learned they play roles in starting the resting state, changing cell membranes, and making sure the resting state forms properly. Two of these new genes seem to help the bacteria talk to each other during this process. One gene helps start a specific part of the bacteria in the resting state, while another helps another part form correctly.

4961038:
In this study, scientists created a way to make mice get lung cancer by using a specific gene mutation. They then tested different medicines on these cancers. One medicine, which stops two types of growth signals in cells, worked well to shrink the cancers caused by the PI3K mutation. But for cancers caused by a different gene mutation, they found that combining the first medicine with another one, which stops a different growth signal, worked much better. This suggests that certain medicines might help treat cancers with specific gene changes.

5152028:
Folic acid helps blood vessels work better in people with narrowed heart arteries without much change in a certain chemical. BACKGROUND Having too much of a chemical called homocysteine might make heart arteries narrower, but it's not clear if having less homocysteine actually prevents this. If reducing homocysteine could help, we know that taking folic acid can lower it by about 25%. To do this, you need to take about 0.4 mg of folic acid each day. Some studies suggest that taking more folic acid, like 5 mg each day, can improve how well blood vessels work, but it's not clear why. One idea is that it might lower the amount of homocysteine in the blood. We wanted to see if folic acid could improve blood vessel health even before changing the amount of homocysteine. METHODS AND RESULTS We did a study where 33 people with narrowed heart arteries took 5 mg of folic acid each day for 6 weeks. We checked how well their blood vessels worked using a test called FMD before they started taking folic acid, and again 2 and 4 hours after the first dose, and after 6 weeks. After just one hour of taking folic acid, the level of a vitamin called folate in their blood went up a lot. Their blood vessels worked better 2 hours after taking folic acid, and even better 4 hours later. But the amount of homocysteine in their blood didn't change much during these early hours.

5289038:
Understanding how the body fights malaria inside the body using the effective spread measure. The immune system and running out of red blood cells help control malaria levels, which can make the illness worse or better and spread more or less. It's hard to figure out exactly how much each does over time. Using information from mouse malaria, we calculated the effective spread measure, which shows how different body defenses work together as the amount of malaria changes. We found that the body's first line of defense against malaria gets less effective as the amount of malaria increases. Also, boosting the body's first defense can indirectly lower malaria levels by making it harder for the malaria to find enough red blood cells. This way of looking at things helps us understand how different parts of the body work together to fight malaria, which could lead to better medicines or vaccines for people.

5304891:
In healthy people, our bodies react differently to germs like bacteria and fungi. We wanted to understand these differences and how genes might play a role. We studied 197 people from Europe who took part in a big health project. We looked at how their blood cells reacted to different types of germs over several years. We noticed that the reactions were mostly related to the type of germ, not to specific parts of the immune system. We also found that certain genes could affect how much of certain chemicals, called cytokines, were made in response to these germs. One gene, in particular, seemed to strongly influence how much of a chemical called IL-6 was made when people were exposed to many different types of germs. This gene might also be linked to getting sick from certain fungal infections. Our findings show that there can be big differences in how our immune systems respond to germs, and some of these differences are due to our genes.

5373138:
In this study, we explore how human embryonic stem cells organize their genetic material in three dimensions. We focused on two types of these cells: naive and primed. To understand this organization, we looked at parts of the DNA that help turn genes on or off, called enhancers and insulators. We used a technique to see where proteins called cohesin attach to the DNA and form loops. These loops create sections of the DNA that are separated from each other, like insulated rooms. These sections then group together to form larger areas, similar to how rooms in a house are grouped into different parts of the building. As the cells change from one type to another, the way these sections interact changes too. We also found that certain parts of the DNA, marked by a protein called CTCF, are important and stay the same across different species. These parts can affect how genes are turned on or off and are often changed in cancer cells. This study helps us understand how the shape of our genetic material affects how genes work, which is important for studying how we develop and what goes wrong in diseases.

5476778: Autoimmune reactions caused by molecular mimicry might lead to neurological diseases. One idea is that when your body gets infected, it sometimes mistakes parts of itself for the infection and attacks them, causing problems. This idea has been suggested for conditions like diabetes, lupus, and multiple sclerosis. But there's not much proof showing exactly what causes these autoimmune reactions.
5483793:
Altered recognition of a specific virus or foreign substance is how some immune cells called CD8+ T cells become less active against cancer in the body. In this study, we found that another type of cell called myeloid-derived suppressor cells (MDSCs) can make CD8+ T cells less able to recognize and fight cancer by changing how they stick together. Specifically, MDSCs cause a chemical change called nitration on parts of the CD8+ T cells that normally help them stick to cancer-fighting molecules. This change stops the CD8+ T cells from being able to properly stick to these molecules and fight the cancer, even though they can still respond to other types of stimulation. The nitration happens when MDSCs produce too much of certain chemicals during close contact with CD8+ T cells. We think this chemical change might affect how the CD8+ T cells move and interact with cancer-fighting molecules. Our findings reveal a new way that MDSCs can make CD8+ T cells less effective at fighting cancer, which could be important for understanding and treating many diseases where MDSCs are present in large numbers.
